verses.text: Furthermore David the king said unto all the congregation, Solomon my son, whom alone God hath chosen, is yet young and tender, and the work is great: for the palace is not for man, but for the LORD God. Now I have prepared with all my might for the house of my God the gold for things to be made of gold, and the silver for things of silver, and the brass for things of brass, the iron for things of iron, and wood for things of wood; onyx stones, and stones to be set, glistering stones, and of divers colours, and all manner of precious stones, and marble stones in abundance. Moreover, because I have set my affection to the house of my God, I have of mine own proper good, of gold and silver, which I have given to the house of my God, over and above all that I have prepared for the holy house, Even three thousand talents of gold, of the gold of Ophir, and seven thousand talents of refined silver, to overlay the walls of the houses withal: The gold for things of gold, and the silver for things of silver, and for all manner of work to be made by the hands of artificers. And who then is willing to consecrate his service this day unto the LORD? Guidance: As David nears the end, he makes a final, moving appeal to the people for the building of the temple — and he leads not merely with words but with sacrificial example. He has prepared vast resources for the house of God, and then, 'because I have set my affection to the house of my God,' he gives from his own personal treasure — three thousand talents of the gold of Ophir and seven thousand of refined silver — over and above all the rest. Then he turns to the people: 'And who then is willing to consecrate his service this day unto the LORD?' The chapter is showing us a leader who gives before he asks, who proves his devotion with costly personal generosity, and only then calls others to give. The chapter is teaching us that true leadership in the things of God leads by example; David did not command the people to do what he was unwilling to do himself, but gave lavishly from his own wealth first. The chapter is teaching us the beauty of setting our 'affection' on the house and worship of God — David gave so freely because he loved God's house with all his heart; generous giving flows from a heart that loves the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the work of God deserves our best and our utmost: David prepared 'with all my might' and gave of his 'own proper good.' Half-hearted, leftover giving is unworthy of the God we serve. And the chapter is teaching us, in David's stirring question, that the LORD invites our willing consecration: 'who then is willing to consecrate his service this day unto the LORD?' God seeks willing hearts, glad and voluntary devotion, not grudging duty. The chapter is teaching us to give generously and gladly to the work of God, to set our affection on His house, and to consecrate ourselves and our substance willingly to the LORD — for the palace is 'not for man, but for the LORD God,' and nothing is too good for Him.

Then the chief of the fathers and princes of the tribes of Israel, and the captains of thousands and of hundreds, with the rulers of the king's work, offered willingly, And gave for the service of the house of God of gold five thousand talents and ten thousand drams, and of silver ten thousand talents, and of brass eighteen thousand talents, and one hundred thousand talents of iron. And they with whom precious stones were found gave them to the treasure of the house of the LORD, by the hand of Jehiel the Gershonite. Then the people rejoiced, for that they offered willingly, because with perfect heart they offered willingly to the LORD: and David the king also rejoiced with great joy. Guidance: David's example kindles the hearts of the people, and the leaders pour out their gifts — gold, silver, brass, iron, precious stones — in staggering abundance. And the Chronicler dwells on the spirit in which they gave: 'the people rejoiced, for that they offered willingly, because with perfect heart they offered willingly to the LORD: and David the king also rejoiced with great joy.' Three times in a breath he stresses that they gave willingly, and twice that they rejoiced. The chapter is showing us the contagious power of generous example and the deep joy of wholehearted giving. The chapter is teaching us that the giving God loves is willing giving — 'God loveth a cheerful giver' (2 Cor 9:7); these gifts were precious not merely for their great value but because they came from a 'perfect heart,' offered gladly and freely to the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that true generosity springs from the heart and overflows in joy; the people 'rejoiced' to give, and their joy was matched by the king's. There is no greater gladness than the gladness of giving to God from a willing heart. The chapter is teaching us that when leaders set a godly example, the people follow with glad and abundant devotion; David gave first, and the whole nation rose to give willingly after him. The chapter is teaching us that the measure of an offering, in God's eyes, is not only its amount but its willingness and the heart behind it. The chapter is teaching us to give to the LORD not grudgingly or of necessity, but cheerfully, freely, with a perfect heart — and to discover, as Israel did that day, that such giving is not loss but joy. The whole congregation rejoiced because they had given willingly to God; let us learn that the open, glad hand is the happy hand, and that the heart most full of cheerful generosity to God is the heart most full of joy.

Wherefore David blessed the LORD before all the congregation: and David said, Blessed be thou, LORD God of Israel our father, for ever and ever. Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory, and the victory, and the majesty: for all that is in the heaven and in the earth is thine; thine is the kingdom, O LORD, and thou art exalted as head above all. Both riches and honour come of thee, and thou reignest over all; and in thine hand is power and might; and in thine hand it is to make great, and to give strength unto all. Now therefore, our God, we thank thee, and praise thy glorious name. Guidance: Moved by the great offering, David breaks out into one of the most magnificent doxologies in all of Scripture: 'Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory, and the victory, and the majesty: for all that is in the heaven and in the earth is thine; thine is the kingdom, O LORD, and thou art exalted as head above all.' He ascribes everything — greatness, power, glory, kingdom, riches, honour, might — entirely to God. The chapter is showing us the heart of true worship: the lifting up of God as supreme over all, the glad confession that all greatness and dominion belong to Him alone. The chapter is teaching us how to praise God — by exalting His attributes and His sovereignty, declaring that the kingdom is His and He is 'exalted as head above all.' These very words echo through the ages and into the Lord's Prayer ('thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory'), teaching the church to crown God with the same adoration David offered. The chapter is teaching us that all that is in heaven and earth is the LORD's, that He reigns over all, and that even 'riches and honour come of thee' — there is nothing we have or are that does not flow from His hand. The chapter is teaching us that it is God 'to make great, and to give strength unto all'; whatever greatness or strength any creature possesses is His gift. The chapter is teaching us to respond to His goodness as David did: 'we thank thee, and praise thy glorious name.' When God has blessed us, when His people have given willingly, when His work goes forward, the fitting response is not pride but praise — to turn from the gift to the Giver and ascribe to Him all the glory. Let us learn from David to make much of God, to exalt Him as head above all, and to confess with full hearts that the greatness, the power, the glory, and the kingdom are His forever.

The chapter is teaching us to respond to His goodness as David did: 'we thank thee, and praise thy glorious name.' When God has blessed us, when His people have given willingly, when His work goes forward, the fitting response is not pride but praise — to turn from the gift to the Giver and ascribe to Him all the glory. Let us learn from David to make much of God, to exalt Him as head above all, and to confess with full hearts that the greatness, the power, the glory, and the kingdom are His forever.

But who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able to offer so willingly after this sort? for all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee. For we are strangers before thee, and sojourners, as were all our fathers: our days on the earth are as a shadow, and there is none abiding. O LORD our God, all this store that we have prepared to build thee an house for thine holy name cometh of thine hand, and is all thine own. I know also, my God, that thou triest the heart, and hast pleasure in uprightness. As for me, in the uprightness of mine heart I have willingly offered all these things: and now have I seen with joy thy people, which are present here, to offer willingly unto thee. Guidance: David's prayer deepens into profound humility and one of the great truths of stewardship: 'who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able to offer so willingly... for all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee.' The vast gifts they had brought were nothing but God's own, given back to Him. And David sees the brevity of life: 'we are strangers before thee, and sojourners... our days on the earth are as a shadow, and there is none abiding.' The chapter is showing us the right spirit even in our most generous moments — not pride in what we give, but wonder that we are allowed to give at all, since all we have is His. The chapter is teaching us the foundational truth of all stewardship: 'all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee.' We can give God nothing that He did not first give us; our offerings are merely returning to Him a portion of His own bounty. This cuts off all boasting and fills us with humble gratitude. The chapter is teaching us the pilgrim character of our lives: we are 'strangers and sojourners,' our days 'as a shadow,' with no abiding home here — a truth that should loosen our grip on earthly treasure and free us to give it gladly to God, since we cannot keep it anyway. The chapter is teaching us that God 'triest the heart, and hast pleasure in uprightness' — He looks not merely at the gift but at the integrity of the giver; what delights Him is an upright heart that gives 'in the uprightness of mine heart.' And the chapter is teaching us the joy of a leader who rejoices not in his own giving only but in the willing devotion of God's people: 'now have I seen with joy thy people... offer willingly unto thee.' Let us give as those who own nothing and owe everything, humbly returning God's own to Him, mindful that our days are a shadow, glad to be counted worthy to offer anything at all to the God from whom and to whom are all things.

O LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, our fathers, keep this for ever in the imagination of the thoughts of the heart of thy people, and prepare their heart unto thee: And give unto Solomon my son a perfect heart, to keep thy commandments, thy testimonies, and thy statutes, and to do all these things, and to build the palace, for the which I have made provision. Guidance: David ends his prayer by interceding for the people and for his son. For the people he prays, 'keep this for ever in the imagination of the thoughts of the heart of thy people, and prepare their heart unto thee' — that this glad, willing devotion might not be a passing mood but a settled disposition, preserved by God. For Solomon he prays, 'give unto Solomon my son a perfect heart, to keep thy commandments... and to build the palace.' The chapter is showing us a godly father and leader whose deepest concern is the spiritual state of those he leaves behind — that their hearts be kept toward God and prepared for Him. The chapter is teaching us how to pray for others, and especially for the next generation: not merely for their outward success but for their hearts — that God would 'prepare their heart' and give them 'a perfect heart' to keep His commandments. The chapter is teaching us that even willing devotion must be sustained by God; David knew that the people's good frame of heart could fade, and so he prayed that God would 'keep this for ever' in them. Our best resolutions need God's keeping to endure. The chapter is teaching us that the great thing to ask for our children and successors is 'a perfect heart, to keep thy commandments' — a heart wholly devoted to God, obedient to His word. David had given Solomon a kingdom, a temple plan, and vast provision, but he knew the one thing needful was a heart right with God, and that only God could give it. The chapter is teaching us to be intercessors for those who come after us, pleading that the LORD would prepare their hearts toward Himself and grant them wholehearted obedience. The richest legacy we can leave is not gold for the temple but prayers that God would give the next generation a perfect heart toward Him.

And David said to all the congregation, Now bless the LORD your God. And all the congregation blessed the LORD God of their fathers, and bowed down their heads, and worshipped the LORD, and the king. And they sacrificed sacrifices unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings unto the LORD, on the morrow after that day, even a thousand bullocks, a thousand rams, and a thousand lambs, with their drink offerings, and sacrifices in abundance for all Israel: And did eat and drink before the LORD on that day with great gladness. And they made Solomon the son of David king the second time, and anointed him unto the LORD to be the chief governor, and Zadok to be priest. Then Solomon sat on the throne of the LORD as king instead of David his father, and prospered; and all Israel obeyed him. And all the princes, and the mighty men, and all the sons likewise of king David, submitted themselves unto Solomon the king. And the LORD magnified Solomon exceedingly in the sight of all Israel, and bestowed upon him such royal majesty as had not been on any king before him in Israel. Guidance: The great assembly ends in worship, sacrifice, feasting, and the peaceful enthronement of Solomon. The people bless the LORD, bow down and worship, offer a thousand bullocks, rams, and lambs, and 'eat and drink before the LORD on that day with great gladness.' Then they make Solomon king 'the second time' and anoint him 'unto the LORD to be the chief governor' — and 'Solomon sat on the throne of the LORD as king,' all Israel obeying him, all the princes and even David's other sons submitting, and the LORD magnifying him exceedingly. The chapter is showing us a smooth, joyful, God-honoured succession — the very opposite of the strife that so often attends the transfer of power; here all is gladness, worship, and willing submission. The chapter is teaching us that when God's order is followed and His people are devoted to Him, transitions of leadership can be marked by peace, joy, and unity rather than rivalry. The chapter is teaching us the striking phrase 'the throne of the LORD' — for Solomon's throne was not merely his own but the LORD's; the king of Israel sat as God's vice-regent, ruling on behalf of the true King. This reminds us that all rightful authority is held under God and answerable to Him. The chapter is teaching us that 'the LORD magnified Solomon exceedingly' — it was God who exalted him, God who gave him majesty beyond all before him; human greatness is the gift of God. The chapter is teaching us, in the willing submission of princes, mighty men, and David's own sons, the beauty of a people united under the king God has appointed — a foreshadowing of the day when every knee shall bow to great David's greater Son, who sits on the throne of the LORD forever. Let us worship with gladness, submit willingly to God's appointed King, and rejoice that the LORD magnifies and establishes whom He will, for the throne is His and the glory is His.

Thus David the son of Jesse reigned over all Israel. And the time that he reigned over Israel was forty years; seven years reigned he in Hebron, and thirty and three years reigned he in Jerusalem. And he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour: and Solomon his son reigned in his stead. Now the acts of David the king, first and last, behold, they are written in the book of Samuel the seer, and in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the book of Gad the seer, With all his reign and his might, and the times that went over him, and over Israel, and over all the kingdoms of the countries. Guidance: The Chronicler closes the life of David: forty years he reigned, seven in Hebron and thirty-three in Jerusalem, 'and he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour: and Solomon his son reigned in his stead.' So ends the story of the shepherd boy whom God took from the sheepcote and made king over Israel — a life that, for all its failures and sorrows, finished well, full and honoured, with the kingdom secure and the temple provided for. The chapter is showing us the peaceful, honourable end of a life lived, in the main, for God; David died 'full of days, riches, and honour,' his work done, his successor on the throne. The chapter is teaching us that a life devoted to God, though not without sin and chastening, can end in fullness and peace; David finished his course, and the LORD gave him a good old age. We are taught to aim, by God's grace, to finish well — to come to the end 'full of days' and at rest in God's faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes outlast any single life: David died, 'and Solomon his son reigned in his stead' — the work of God went on, the temple would be built, the line would continue toward the promised Son. No servant of God is indispensable; the LORD carries His purposes from generation to generation. The chapter is teaching us, in the mention of the books of Samuel, Nathan, and Gad, that David's acts were faithfully recorded — that a life lived before God leaves a testimony for those who follow. The chapter is teaching us to live so that our end may be peaceful and our memory a blessing, trusting that the God who was with David from the sheepcote to the throne will be with His people from first to last, and will carry forward His kingdom long after we are gone, until the everlasting King, the Son of David, reigns over all. So the first book of Chronicles ends — not in death's despair, but in the peace of a finished life and the sure continuance of the purposes of God.

Thus David the son of Jesse reigned over all Israel. And the time that he reigned over Israel was forty years; seven years reigned he in Hebron, and thirty and three years reigned he in Jerusalem. And he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour: and Solomon his son reigned in his stead. Now the acts of David the king, first and last, behold, they are written in the book of Samuel the seer, and in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the book of Gad the seer, With all his reign and his might, and the times that went over him, and over Israel, and over all the kingdoms of the countries.
